passage: In 1905, Albert Einstein published not one but four papers, any one of which would have secured his scientific reputation. The first explained Brownian motion; the second proposed the photon as a quantum of light; the third introduced special relativity; and the fourth derived the equation E = mc². Einstein was then an unknown twenty-six-year-old patent clerk with no academic position. The year is now called Einstein's annus mirabilis—his 'miracle year.'
question: What is the primary purpose of this text?

choices.A: To argue that Einstein's productivity in 1905 resulted from his unusual working conditions as a patent clerk
choices.B: To explain the scientific content of Einstein's four 1905 papers in detail
choices.C: To highlight the extraordinary achievement of Einstein's 1905 papers and the remarkable circumstances under which they were produced
choices.D: To compare Einstein's 1905 publications with those of other physicists working at the same time

explanation: The text accomplishes two things: it catalogs Einstein's extraordinary scientific output in 1905 (four papers, any one of which would have secured his reputation) and emphasizes the remarkable circumstances (an unknown 26-year-old with no academic position). The concluding phrase 'miracle year' reinforces this sense of wonder. Choice C accurately captures both the achievement and the circumstances. Choice A introduces a causal argument about the patent clerk role that the text does not make—it mentions his job but does not argue it caused his productivity. Choice B is incorrect; the text only names the papers, not their scientific content in detail. Choice D introduces a comparison with other physicists that the text never makes.
